data.source: Laboratory/field experiments with participants recruited on campus at the University of Toulouse
data.sample_size: Experiment 1: 85 participants (59 women). Experiment 2: 115 participants initially; final analytic sample 110 after removal of 5 multivariate outliers.
data.notes: Participants were randomly assigned to experimental conditions. No public data repository is indicated.

method.description: Two experiments examined whether reminding individuals of their own mortality (mortality salience) reduces utilitarian moral judgments. Experiment 1 compared Death-thought and Pain-thought conditions on responses to moral conflict versus control scenarios. Experiment 2 compared the mortality-salience effect with three levels of concurrent cognitive load (low, high, very high) using a dot-matrix memory task.
steps: Recruit university participants and obtain consent.
Randomly assign participants to experimental conditions (Death vs. Pain thought in Experiment 1; Death vs. Pain thought crossed with Low/High/Very-high cognitive load in Experiment 2).
Administer mortality-salience (or pain) writing manipulation: participants answer two open-ended questions about their own death (or extreme pain).
Present moral dilemmas (two scenarios, each in conflict and control versions) and record judgments of moral acceptability of the utilitarian action.
In Experiment 2, before each dilemma display a dot-matrix (3×3 easy; 3×3 difficult; or 4×4 very difficult) for memorization and later reproduction to impose cognitive load.
Compute percentage of utilitarian responses per participant and condition.
Analyze data with ANOVAs and planned t-tests on utilitarian-response percentages (factors: problem type, mortality-salience condition, cognitive load) and report effect sizes.
method.models: Analysis of Variance (ANOVA) with interaction terms; independent-samples t-tests
method.outcome_variable: Percentage of utilitarian responses to moral dilemmas
method.independent_variables: Mortality-salience condition (Death vs. Pain); Problem type (Conflict vs. Control); Cognitive load level (Low, High, Very High; Experiment 2 only)
method.control_variables: Scenario order counterbalanced; gender and age not used as covariates in reported analyses

results.summary: Across both experiments, utilitarian responses were less frequent for conflict dilemmas than control dilemmas. Reminders of mortality significantly reduced utilitarian judgments on conflict dilemmas but not on control dilemmas (Experiment 1). In Experiment 2, mortality salience again reduced utilitarian responses on conflict dilemmas, and an analogous reduction occurred only under the very-high cognitive-load condition, suggesting that mortality salience consumes substantial cognitive resources. No interaction between mortality salience and cognitive load was observed.

numerical_results.outcome_name: Interaction effect (Problem type × Mortality salience) on utilitarian responses, Experiment 1
Mortality-salience effect on conflict problems, Experiment 1
Main effect of Mortality salience on conflict problems, Experiment 2
Main effect of Cognitive load on conflict problems, Experiment 2
Percentage of utilitarian responses, Conflict problems, Very-high load vs. Low load (Pain condition), Experiment 2

metadata.original_paper_title: Mortality salience and morality: Thinking about death makes people less utilitarian
